summary: McKenzie describes how a simple CrazyEgg heatmap analysis led him to add a second download button on his main page, resulting in a 20% conversion increase. After noticing he hadn't used his $20/month CrazyEgg subscription in months, he identified that users reading his benefits list had no nearby call-to-action. Adding one button captured 10.4% of page clicks and translated to roughly $160/month in additional revenue. The post illustrates how small, data-driven UX changes yield outsized business results.
key_points: A single additional download button placed after the benefits list captured 10.4% of all page clicks from 4,200 visitors
The change increased main page conversion by approximately 20%, translating to an estimated $160/month in added revenue
Users who scroll past content to read benefits need an immediate call-to-action rather than being forced to scroll further or backtrack
The first text link in a content area gets a disproportionate share of user interaction and should point to the conversion goal
Even experienced practitioners neglect obvious optimizations when they stop regularly reviewing their analytics
money_quotes: 86 clicks, or 10.4% of clicks on the page, on that one stinking extra button.
This increases the conversion of my main page by about 20% (some clicks are presumably "stolen" from other ways to reach the same goal).
The naive assumption is that 20% more trials is 20% more sales.
Putting a button there is kind of jawdroppingly obvious… I'm not sure why I didn't do it before.
The first text link in the content area gets a huge portion of user interaction.
If someone scrolls down to actually read the benefits list, then they're left high and dry with nowhere to click unless they scroll past the huge image or scroll upwards.
Neither of those activities "flows" well after you've just read a benefits list, not nearly as well as "click here" does.
key_insight: Placing a call-to-action immediately after your persuasive content — rather than forcing users to scroll or backtrack — is an obvious but commonly neglected optimization that can meaningfully increase conversions.
